summary.objective: The paper investigates how geometric disorder from random obstacles on a 2D square lattice controls collective chase-and-escape dynamics.
summary.methods: Using an agent-based model with short-range sensing rules, the study combines Breadth-First Search for geometric connectivity, trapping time and capture cost metrics, survival analysis with Weibull distribution fits, and mean-squared displacement exponent characterization.
summary.results: Non-monotonic trapping time and cost versus obstacle density, a crossover in survival statistics from compressed to stretched exponential decay, and subdiffusive transport converging near the percolation threshold reveal a geometry-driven crossover linked to lattice connectivity.
summary.conclusion: Percolation and connectivity serve as unifying control parameters, demonstrating that chase-escape dynamics in disordered environments are governed by a geometry-driven crossover affecting spatial, temporal, and collective behavior.
